questions.question: A logistics hub can process 1,000 air-freight parcels or 200 ocean-freight containers per shift. The hub increases container processing by 40 units. What is the opportunity cost in air-freight parcels?
options: 40 parcels, matching the increase in container processing directly
200 parcels, reflecting the opportunity cost ratio of five to one
400 parcels, representing half of the total container capacity
1,000 parcels, equal to the entire air-freight processing capacity
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 container = 1,000 ÷ 200 = 5 parcels. For 40 additional containers: 40 × 5 = 200 parcels sacrificed.

questions.question: Worker Alpha harvests 30 bushels of apples or 10 bushels of pears per day. Worker Beta harvests 20 bushels of apples or 8 bushels of pears. Who has the absolute advantage in pear production?
options: Worker Beta, because pears are a smaller share of Beta's total daily output
Worker Alpha, because Alpha harvests more bushels of pears per day than Beta
Worker Beta, because Beta's apple and pear outputs are closer to each other
Neither worker, because both produce pears at a different rate than apples
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage compares gross output. Alpha harvests 10 bushels of pears vs. Beta's 8 bushels per day.

questions.question: Country A produces a car in 100 hours and a bike in 5 hours. Country B produces a car in 150 hours and a bike in 10 hours. Which country has the comparative advantage in bikes?
options: Country B, because it produces cars in more hours, freeing workers for bikes
Country A, because its opportunity cost of bikes is lower at 0.05 cars per bike
Country B, because its bike production time is twice its car production ratio
Country A, because it produces both cars and bikes in fewer total labor hours
questions.explanation: Country A's opportunity cost of 1 bike = 5/100 = 0.05 cars. Country B's = 10/150 = 0.066 cars. Country A has the lower relative cost in bikes.

questions.question: An economy moves from a point inside its Production Possibility Frontier to a point exactly on the boundary. What does this movement represent?
options: An outward shift of the frontier due to improved production technology
The elimination of productive inefficiency through full resource utilization
A change in consumer preferences toward one good and away from the other
An increase in the total factor endowment available to the national economy
questions.explanation: Moving to the PPF boundary means previously idle or misallocated resources are now being used to their maximum productive potential.

questions.question: Country 1 has an absolute advantage in both Goods X and Y. Its productivity lead is 2 times in Good X and 3 times in Good Y. Which good should Country 1 export?
options: Good X, because its productivity lead in X is smaller and easier to defend
Good Y, because Country 1's greatest relative productivity advantage is in Y
Both goods equally, because absolute advantage applies to all production
Neither good, because trading requires each country to lead in one product
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage follows the largest relative productivity gap. With a 3× lead in Y vs. 2× in X, Country 1's comparative advantage lies in Good Y.

questions.question: The world relative price (P_X/P_Y) is higher than both countries' domestic opportunity costs in the Ricardian model. What is the global supply of Good Y?
options: Maximum possible, because both countries produce Y for the high world price
Zero, because both countries fully specialize in producing only Good X
Half of each country's capacity, split evenly between the two goods
Indeterminate, because the model cannot predict output at extreme prices
questions.explanation: If the price of X is high relative to Y for everyone, neither country finds it profitable to produce Y at all. Both fully specialize in Good X.

questions.question: The World Relative Demand curve for Good X lies far to the right in a two-country Ricardian model. Where is the equilibrium price most likely to settle?
options: At the opportunity cost of the country with the comparative advantage in X
At the internal opportunity cost of the country with the comparative disadvantage in X
At the midpoint between the two countries' domestic opportunity cost ratios
Below both domestic costs so that consumers in both nations benefit equally
questions.explanation: Very high demand for X forces the country that would normally specialize in Y to begin producing X as well, pushing the price up to its higher opportunity cost.

questions.question: Country A has productivity of 20 with a nominal wage of \$40. Country B has productivity of 5 with a nominal wage of \$5. Which country has the lower unit labor cost?
options: Country A, because its absolute productivity is four times higher than Country B's
Country B, with a unit labor cost of \$1 compared to Country A's cost of \$2
Both countries have equal unit labor costs of \$2 per unit of output produced
Country A, because higher wages always signal greater cost competitiveness
questions.explanation: Country A unit cost = \$40/20 = \$2. Country B unit cost = \$5/5 = \$1. Country B is more cost-competitive despite lower absolute productivity.

questions.question: According to the critique of the pauper labor argument, why is trade between high-wage and low-wage countries not harmful to the high-wage country?
options: Low-wage countries are always too small to affect high-wage country markets
High wages reflect high productivity, so unit labor costs can remain competitive
International trade law requires low-wage countries to raise wages over time
High-wage countries can always use tariffs to block low-wage import competition
questions.explanation: Trade depends on comparative advantage. High wages generally accompany high productivity, keeping unit labor costs balanced and trade mutually beneficial.

questions.question: How does international trade affect the Consumption Possibilities Frontier of a small nation in the Ricardian model?
options: The CPF contracts as domestic firms face import competition from abroad
The CPF expands outward beyond the limits of the domestic Production Possibility Frontier
The CPF remains identical to the PPF because consumption must equal production
The CPF shifts inward as the country specializes in a narrower range of goods
questions.explanation: Trade allows the nation to consume combinations of goods that it could not produce on its own, expanding consumption beyond the production frontier.

questions.question: A country discovers new technology that doubles productivity in its import-competing sector while the export sector remains unchanged. What happens to its gains from trade?
options: The gains from trade increase because the country now produces more overall
The gains from trade decrease because the relative benefit of importing shrinks
The gains are unaffected because productivity only matters in the export sector
The gains double, matching the productivity improvement in the import sector
questions.explanation: As domestic efficiency in the import sector rises, the indirect production benefit of trade (importing instead of producing domestically) becomes relatively less valuable.

questions.question: A government policy makes the rich much richer and the poor only slightly richer, with no one losing income. Is this outcome a Pareto Improvement?
options: No, because Pareto Improvements require that benefits be distributed equally
Yes, because at least some are better off and absolutely no one is worse off
No, because the policy increases income inequality, which is socially undesirable
Yes, but only if the rich voluntarily share their gains with the poorer group
questions.explanation: A Pareto Improvement requires only that at least one person gains and no one loses. The distribution of gains is irrelevant to the Pareto criterion.

questions.question: Why does a tax imposed on a good with no externalities create a Pareto Inefficient outcome in the market?
options: Taxes always reduce government revenue below the level needed for public goods
The tax creates a wedge between the consumer price and the producer price received
Taxes on goods are always regressive and hurt low-income consumers the most
A tax with no externalities is illegal under international trade agreement rules
questions.explanation: The tax wedge prevents the marginal benefit to consumers from equaling the marginal cost of production, causing lost trades that would have benefited both parties.

questions.question: P_Good1 = \$60, a_L1 = 4, P_Good2 = \$40, a_L2 = 2. With perfectly mobile labor, which sector will a rational worker choose to enter?
options: Sector 1, because the price per unit of Good 1 is higher than Good 2
Sector 2, because the hourly wage of \$20 exceeds Sector 1's wage of \$15
Sector 1, because producing fewer units per hour signals higher skill requirements
The worker is indifferent because total revenue is equal in both sectors
questions.explanation: Wage in Sector 1 = \$60/4 = \$15. Wage in Sector 2 = \$40/2 = \$20. Rational workers migrate to the higher-paying Sector 2.

questions.question: What is the primary constraint that limits total production in the Ricardian model of trade?
options: The world relative price ratio established through international exchange
The total labor endowment, since labor is the only factor of production
The available stock of capital that workers need to produce any output
The level of technology, which is assumed to differ between trading nations
questions.explanation: With labor as the sole input, production is limited by a_LX × Q_X + a_LY × Q_Y ≤ L, where L is the total labor force.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model under autarky, a country's total labor force suddenly doubles. What happens to the domestic relative price ratio?
options: The ratio falls by half because more labor reduces the cost of all goods
The ratio remains unchanged because it depends only on unit labor requirements
The ratio doubles because twice as many workers can now produce both goods
The ratio becomes indeterminate as the PPF shifts outward in all directions
questions.explanation: Autarky relative prices equal the ratio of unit labor requirements (a_LX / a_LY). This ratio is independent of the total quantity of labor available.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the government imposes a new tariff that raises the domestic price of an import-competing good. Which group benefits from this policy?
options: All factors across every sector of the domestic economy benefit equally
Factors of production that are specific to the protected import-competing sector
Only foreign exporters who can now charge higher prices in the domestic market
Consumers, because the tariff generates government revenue for public services
questions.explanation: With factor immobility, the price increase benefits only those trapped in the protected sector. Their nominal income rises while other prices remain stable.

questions.question: Why is the immobile factor model classified as a short-run model of international trade?
options: Because the model only applies to countries during their first year of trading
In the long run, factors like labor and capital can eventually move between industries
Short-run models assume that all goods prices are completely rigid and fixed
The model was developed to analyze only temporary tariff policies, not permanent ones
questions.explanation: The model captures the immediate friction and adjustment costs of trade shocks before factors have time to retrain, relocate, or be repurposed.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, how does trade affect factor returns in the export sector compared to the import-competing sector?
options: Returns converge to a single uniform level across both sectors of the economy
Returns diverge, with export-sector factors earning more while import-sector factors lose
Returns remain equal because factor prices are set in competitive national markets
Returns fall in both sectors as trade reduces the overall domestic price level
questions.explanation: Since factors cannot move to equalize returns, those in the expanding export sector thrive while those trapped in the shrinking import sector experience declining incomes.

questions.question: A labor-abundant country exports a labor-intensive good after trade opens in the H-O model. What happens to the wage-to-rental ratio (w/r)?
options: The wage-to-rental ratio decreases as capital becomes relatively more scarce
The wage-to-rental ratio increases as demand for labor-intensive goods rises
The ratio remains constant because factor endowments are fixed in the model
The ratio initially falls but recovers as capital flows in from abroad over time
questions.explanation: Trade increases demand for the labor-intensive export good, raising the derived demand and relative price of the abundant factor, labor. Thus w/r rises.

questions.question: According to the Heckscher-Ohlin model, why do developed nations tend to export high-tech manufactured goods?
options: High-tech goods are always protected by international patent laws in rich countries
Developed nations are relatively abundant in skilled labor and physical capital
Developing nations lack the consumer demand needed for high-tech product markets
WTO rules assign high-tech production exclusively to developed member countries
questions.explanation: High-tech goods are intensive in skilled labor and capital, which developed nations possess in higher ratios relative to developing trading partners.

questions.question: What does the Factor-Price Equalization theorem predict about international trade in the H-O framework?
options: Wage gaps between countries will grow larger as trade expands over time
Free trade in goods will tend to equalize wages and capital returns across nations
Only capital returns converge while wage differences between countries remain permanent
Factor prices are determined entirely by domestic policy, not by international trade
questions.explanation: By trading goods, countries indirectly exchange the factor services embodied in them. This process pulls factor prices toward convergence globally.

questions.question: How does biased growth toward the abundant factor affect the Production Possibility Frontier in the H-O model?
options: The PPF shifts outward uniformly in all directions by the same proportion
The PPF shifts out more in the direction of the good using that factor intensively
The PPF shifts inward for the scarce-factor good while the abundant good expands
Biased growth has no effect on the PPF because growth always affects all goods equally
questions.explanation: Increasing one factor endowment disproportionately expands maximum output of the industry that uses that factor most intensively.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the price of Manufactured goods falls by 5% while agricultural prices remain constant. What happens to the nominal wage?
options: It falls by exactly 5%, matching the price decline in the manufacturing sector
It falls by less than 5%, cushioned by labor moving to the agricultural sector
It remains unchanged because agricultural prices anchor the overall wage level
It falls by more than 5% due to the Magnification Effect on specific factors
questions.explanation: Labor demand in manufacturing shifts down, but as labor exits to agriculture, the marginal product of remaining manufacturing workers rises, cushioning the wage decline.

questions.question: A massive influx of new workers enters the Manufacturing sector in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the rental rate of fixed Manufacturing Capital?
options: The rental rate decreases because more workers dilute the capital per worker
The rental rate of capital increases as more labor raises capital's marginal product
The rental rate remains constant because the capital stock has not changed
The rental rate initially rises but falls as diminishing returns eventually set in
questions.explanation: More workers using the same fixed capital stock increases the marginal productivity of that capital, raising its rental return for capital owners.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the price of Food increases. How are owners of Manufacturing Capital affected?
options: Their real income rises because higher food prices boost the entire economy
Their real income decreases as wages rise and food costs more while their returns are flat
Their real income is unchanged because capital and agricultural land are separate
Their nominal returns rise but their real income falls, making the net effect zero
questions.explanation: Nominal wages rise due to food sector demand pulling labor, and food becomes more expensive, while manufacturing prices and capital returns remain flat. Real income unambiguously falls.

questions.question: Why is the Production Possibility Frontier curved rather than straight in the Specific Factor Model?
options: Because the model assumes constant returns to scale in both sectors
Due to diminishing returns to the mobile factor as it works with fixed specific factors
Because governments impose production quotas that distort the output mix
The PPF is actually straight in the Specific Factor Model, just like in the Ricardian model
questions.explanation: As labor shifts to one sector, it works with a fixed amount of the specific factor. Marginal output decreases, creating a concave PPF with increasing opportunity costs.

questions.question: What defines intra-industry trade in New Trade Theory?
options: Trade between firms that operate in entirely different industrial sectors
The simultaneous export and import of differentiated goods within the same industry
Trade that occurs exclusively within a single multinational corporation's supply chain
The exchange of raw materials from one country for finished goods from another
questions.explanation: Intra-industry trade involves two-way exchange of similar products, such as countries both exporting and importing different varieties of automobiles.

questions.question: Why might a government provide subsidies to support a national champion firm in an industry characterized by very high fixed costs?
options: To ensure the firm can pay above-market wages and attract the best workers
To help the firm achieve enough scale to lower average costs and compete globally
To compensate the firm for paying higher taxes than its foreign competitors do
To meet WTO requirements that all large firms receive some government support
questions.explanation: In industries with significant increasing returns to scale, reaching a large production volume is essential for cost competitiveness. Subsidies can help achieve that scale.

questions.question: How do agglomeration economies help explain why certain industries concentrate in specific cities or regions?
options: Government tax incentives force firms to relocate to designated industrial zones
Geographic clustering creates shared labor pools, suppliers, and knowledge spillovers
Natural resources are distributed unevenly, forcing industries to locate near inputs
Transportation costs are zero in large cities, making any location equally efficient
questions.explanation: External economies of scale arise from proximity. Firms benefit from specialized workers, shared suppliers, and informal exchange of ideas within the cluster.

questions.question: What is the most fundamental difference between an import quota and a tariff as trade policy instruments?
options: Quotas always generate more government revenue than equivalent tariffs do
A quota directly limits the quantity imported, while a tariff works through the price
Tariffs are illegal under WTO rules while quotas are explicitly permitted
Quotas affect only agricultural goods while tariffs apply to manufactured products
questions.explanation: Quotas set a hard physical ceiling on import volume. Tariffs raise the domestic price, indirectly reducing the quantity imported through market mechanisms.

questions.question: A government provides a low-interest loan to a domestic solar panel manufacturer to support its export activities. What type of trade policy is this?
options: A countervailing duty designed to offset foreign government assistance programs
An export subsidy that lowers the firm's effective cost of selling in foreign markets
A domestic content requirement mandating the use of locally sourced components
A tariff-rate quota providing preferential access for domestically made goods
questions.explanation: Any form of financial assistance that reduces the cost for domestic firms to sell abroad qualifies as an export subsidy.

questions.question: Why do most economists argue that a tariff is a more efficient policy instrument than an equivalent import quota?
options: Tariffs are easier to calculate because they use fixed percentages rather than volumes
The government collects the tariff revenue, whereas quota rents often go to private actors
Quotas always restrict more trade than tariffs at the same nominal protection level
Tariffs are permitted under WTO rules while all quotas are strictly prohibited
questions.explanation: Tariff revenue accrues to the public treasury and can offset other distortionary taxes. Quota rents typically go to license holders unless the government auctions the licenses.

questions.question: What is the trade creation effect that can result from forming a Free Trade Area?
options: New industries emerge that did not previously exist in any of the member countries
High-cost domestic production is replaced by lower-cost imports from a member country
Member countries erect higher external tariffs against all non-member trading partners
Trade between members and non-members increases due to faster economic growth
questions.explanation: Trade creation is welfare-enhancing. It occurs when preferential tariff removal shifts sourcing from inefficient domestic producers to more efficient bloc members.

questions.question: A large country imposes a tariff. The world price of the import falls by \$2 while the domestic price rises by \$3. What is the per-unit value of the tariff?
options: \$1, representing the net difference between the two price changes
\$5, equal to the sum of the domestic price increase and the world price decrease
\$3, matching only the increase observed in the domestic market price
\$2, reflecting only the decrease in the world price caused by the tariff
questions.explanation: The tariff equals the new domestic price minus the new world price. Domestic price rises by \$3 and world price falls by \$2, so the tariff is \$3 + \$2 = \$5.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a specific tariff on imported goods?
options: A tax calculated as a fixed percentage of the assessed value of the good
A fixed dollar amount charged for each physical unit imported, regardless of price
A variable rate that increases when import volumes exceed a threshold quantity
A tariff that applies only to goods originating from certain named countries
questions.explanation: A specific tariff is a per-unit charge (e.g., \$10 per ton) that does not vary with the market price or assessed value of the imported good.

questions.question: What is the Metzler Paradox in international trade theory?
options: A tariff always improves the imposing country's welfare regardless of its size
A tariff can actually cause the domestic price of the imported good to fall
Free trade always reduces welfare in countries that possess comparative advantage
Import quotas are always superior to tariffs in maximizing national economic welfare
questions.explanation: The Metzler Paradox is a rare theoretical case where a large country's tariff so severely depresses the world price that the domestic price ends up lower than before the tariff.

questions.question: A country imposes a high tariff on imported steel (an intermediate good) but a low tariff on imported cars (the final good). What happens to the Effective Rate of Protection for domestic car producers?
options: It equals the nominal tariff on cars because input tariffs are a separate policy
It is lower than the nominal car tariff because expensive inputs tax domestic producers
It is higher than the nominal car tariff because steel protection helps car makers
It is exactly zero because the two tariff rates cancel each other out perfectly
questions.explanation: High input costs from steel tariffs act as a tax on domestic car assembly. This reduces value-added per vehicle, making the effective protection lower than the nominal car tariff.

questions.question: A domestic market is protected by a binding import quota. Domestic consumer demand for the good suddenly increases. What is the market outcome?
options: The import volume rises to accommodate the increase in consumer demand
The domestic price rises further while the quantity of imports remains fixed
The domestic price falls as producers expand output to meet the new demand
The quota automatically expands to prevent any shortage in the domestic market
questions.explanation: With the import quantity capped by the quota, any increase in demand must be met by higher domestic prices or expanded domestic production. Import volume cannot rise.

questions.question: How can a Voluntary Export Restraint actually benefit the exporting country that agrees to limit its own shipments?
options: The exporting country receives compensation payments from the importing country
Exporting firms can raise their prices and capture the resulting scarcity rent
VERs allow exporters to avoid paying customs duties in the importing country
The restriction forces domestic firms in the importing country to exit the market
questions.explanation: By voluntarily limiting supply, foreign exporters effectively coordinate like a cartel. The restricted quantity drives up prices, and exporters capture the resulting quota rent.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a Technical Barrier to Trade that could restrict imports?
options: A numerical limit on the total number of cars that can be imported each year
Mandatory safety testing standards that are uniquely difficult for foreign firms to meet
A direct government payment to domestic firms for each unit they export abroad
A tax calculated as a percentage of the value of each imported manufactured good
questions.explanation: Technical regulations like safety or labeling standards can act as non-tariff barriers when designed to favor domestic products or disadvantage foreign competitors.

questions.question: What does the process of trade liberalization involve?
options: Increasing tariffs to protect domestic industries from foreign import competition
Reducing tariffs and removing non-tariff barriers to promote freer international exchange
Negotiating voluntary export restraints with major trading partner countries
Establishing government monopolies over all international trade transactions
questions.explanation: Trade liberalization is the movement toward free trade by lowering tariff rates and eliminating quotas, subsidies, and other barriers to cross-border commerce.

questions.question: A country consistently runs a Current Account surplus. What does this necessarily imply about its relationship with the rest of the world?
options: The country is a net borrower, accumulating debt owed to foreign creditors
The country is a net lender, using excess earnings to acquire foreign assets
The country's Financial Account must also be in surplus by the same amount
The country is depleting its official foreign exchange reserve holdings
questions.explanation: A Current Account surplus means the country earns more than it spends. The excess is used to lend to or invest in the rest of the world.

questions.question: A UK-based electronics firm purchases and builds a new factory in Vietnam. In which Balance of Payments account is this transaction recorded?
options: The Current Account, under the services and goods trade balance category
The Financial Account, classified as Foreign Direct Investment outflow
The Capital Account, which records all non-financial asset transactions
The Official Reserves Account, managed by Vietnam's central monetary authority
questions.explanation: Building a factory abroad is Foreign Direct Investment. FDI involves acquiring physical assets or significant ownership, recorded in the Financial Account.

questions.question: What is the correct national income identity relating Saving, Investment, and the Current Account?
options: CA = I – S, meaning the Current Account equals investment minus saving
S = I + CA, meaning national saving funds investment plus net foreign asset accumulation
I = S – CA, meaning investment equals saving minus the Current Account balance
S + I = CA, meaning saving plus investment determines the Current Account
questions.explanation: National saving can be used either for domestic investment (I) or to acquire net foreign assets, which is the Current Account balance (CA). So S = I + CA.

questions.question: The US government sells a Treasury bond to a Japanese commercial bank. How is this transaction recorded in the US Balance of Payments?
options: As a debit in the Current Account because the US is importing financial capital
As a credit in the Financial Account reflecting an inflow of foreign capital
As a debit in the Financial Account because the US is selling a domestic asset
As a credit in the Current Account under the services and income category
questions.explanation: Selling a domestic asset to a foreigner brings capital into the country. This capital inflow is recorded as a credit in the US Financial Account.

questions.question: The UK interest rate is 5% and the US interest rate is 2%. According to Covered Interest Parity, what should be the approximate forward discount on the British Pound?
options: 7%, reflecting the sum of the two different national interest rates
3%, equal to the interest rate differential between the UK and the US
2%, matching the lower interest rate available on US Dollar assets
5%, because the higher-yielding currency always depreciates by its own rate
questions.explanation: The currency with the higher interest rate (Pound) trades at a forward discount equal to the interest differential (5% – 2% = 3%) to prevent arbitrage.

questions.question: What does a nominal depreciation of a domestic currency mean in foreign exchange markets?
options: The domestic currency can purchase more units of a foreign currency than before
The domestic currency buys fewer units of a foreign currency than it did previously
The real exchange rate has increased while the nominal rate has remained constant
The central bank has officially changed the currency's value by government decree
questions.explanation: Nominal depreciation means the market value of the currency has fallen. It now takes more units of domestic currency to buy one unit of foreign currency.

questions.question: How does a currency board arrangement operate as an exchange rate system?
options: The exchange rate is allowed to float freely with no central bank intervention
The central bank holds 100% foreign reserves backing for the domestic money supply
A committee of commercial bank executives meets regularly to set the exchange rate
Multiple countries agree to share a single common currency and a unified central bank
questions.explanation: A currency board is an extreme fixed-rate regime. The central bank must hold foreign reserves equal to the entire domestic monetary base at the pegged exchange rate.

questions.question: Under a fixed exchange rate system, the domestic currency is currently undervalued relative to its pegged rate. What action must the central bank take to maintain the peg?
options: Sell domestic currency and purchase foreign reserves to increase the money supply
Buy domestic currency using foreign reserves to reduce the supply in the market
Raise domestic interest rates sharply to attract additional foreign capital inflows
Allow the currency to float temporarily until it returns to the pegged value
questions.explanation: An undervalued currency faces upward pressure. To prevent appreciation, the central bank sells domestic currency and buys foreign reserves, increasing the domestic money supply.
